answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical chain locks a visible, bodily ascension. Luke 24:50-51: Jesus eparas (G1869, aorist participle active, lifted up) his cheiras (G5495, hands) and eulogesen (G2127) them while ho diestē (he parted from them); the textual variant including kai anephereto eis ton ouranon (and was carried up into heaven, anapherō G399) is well-attested in NA28. Acts 1:9-11: epērthē (G1869 aorist passive, he was lifted up) blepontōn autōn (while they were watching), nephelē (G3507, cloud) hypelaben (G5274) auton, and the angels say houtos ho Iēsous ho analēmphtheis (G353, perfect passive participle, this same Jesus who has been taken up) houtōs eleusetai hon tropon (G5158, in the same manner) etheasasthe (G2300, you beheld) auton poreuomenon (G4198) eis ton ouranon. The visible mode of departure is named as the template for the visible mode of return. Eph 4:8-10: anabas eis hypsos (G305, G5311) and anabas hyperanō pantōn tōn ouranōn (far above all the heavens, G5231), contrasted with the prior katebē (G2597, descended). 1 Tim 3:16 hymn: anelēmphthē en doxē (G353, was taken up in glory). Heb 1:3: ekathisen (G2523, sat down, aorist) en dexia (G1188, right hand) tēs megalōsynēs (G3172, Majesty) en hypsēlois (G5308, on high), with the same seating motif reiterated Heb 10:12, Heb 12:2, Mark 16:19, Acts 2:33-34, Acts 7:55-56, Eph 1:20, Col 3:1, 1 Pet 3:22. The lemma analambanō (G353) carries Christ-ascent in Mark 16:19, Acts 1:2, Acts 1:11, Acts 1:22, 1 Tim 3:16: five distinct authors, one lexical formula.
answer.notes: Bodily ascension is entailed by bodily resurrection (Luke 24:39 flesh and bones) plus the apostolic eyewitness formula (blepontōn autōn, etheasasthe in Acts 1:9-11). Denial typically rides one of two paths: (1) Christ was raised as a spirit, therefore the ascension is invisible or spiritual only (Jehovah's Witnesses, Watchtower Society, Charles Taze Russell lineage; Christadelphian and some Adventist-derived sectors variously); (2) demythologizing readings (Bultmann school) that recast Luke 24 and Acts 1 as mythic packaging of an internal disciple-faith event. The OCA's pastoral framing warns against picturing Christ on a material throne in created space but still affirms the bodily ascent of Christ's humanity into the divine life (theosis frame); this is nuance over the metaphysics of heaven, not denial of the bodily event. Some liberal mainline scholarship treats Acts 1 as Lukan composition; the apparatus and the pan-canonical lemma-network do not require that concession. Denial collapses the apostolic kerygma at multiple points: the angelic interpretation in Acts 1:11 explicitly anchors the parousia to the manner of the ascension, so denial of bodily ascension destabilizes bodily return as well. Confessed by the Apostles' Creed, Nicene Creed, every magisterial Protestant standard, Catholic and Orthodox tradition: standing as a Christological-boundary line is canonically demonstrated, not lineage-counted.

stem_audit.notes: The statement asserts the verdict in its statement (Christ ascended bodily into heaven forty days after His resurrection). However the verdict is the lexical content of the canonical witness itself, testable directly against the apparatus. The forty-day detail comes from Acts 1:3 (di hēmerōn tessarakonta) which is canonical. The alternative reading (spirit-only or invisible ascension) is named and disambiguated inside the canon (Luke 24:39 flesh and bones; Acts 1:9-11 blepontōn autōn, etheasasthe, hon tropon). No neutralization required.

lay_summary.reasoning: The Bible teaches that after Jesus rose physically from the dead, he was taken up bodily into heaven in front of his disciples while they watched, and that this same Jesus will come back the same visible way. The strongest reason is Acts 1:9-11. Luke writes that while the apostles were watching, Jesus was lifted up and a cloud took him from their sight. Then two men in white say to them, why do you stand looking into heaven? This same Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will come in the same way as you saw him go into heaven. Luke 24:50-51 says he led them out to Bethany, lifted up his hands, blessed them, and was carried up into heaven. 1 Timothy 3:16 places the ascension inside an early Christian hymn confessing the gospel. Hebrews 1:3 says he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high after making purification for sins, and Ephesians 4:8-10 contrasts his descent with his ascent far above all the heavens so he might fill all things. The strongest complicating question is the physics. Where exactly is heaven, and does the ascension require Jesus to keep traveling upward forever through space? The biblical writers are not answering a physics question. They are saying that the same risen body that the disciples touched and that ate fish in front of them was visibly removed from earthly residence and entered the heavenly throne-room of God. The seating-at-the-right-hand language across Hebrews, Mark 16:19, Acts 2:33, Ephesians 1:20, and 1 Peter 3:22 names a status and a place of authority, not a spatial coordinate. The verdict handles the tension by reading the ascent as real, bodily, and visible at its earthly end, while heaven itself is the realm of God where the risen humanity of Jesus is now seated.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every historic Christian lineage confesses the bodily ascension. The Apostles' Creed and the Nicene Creed both state He ascended into heaven and sits at the right hand of the Father. The Catholic Catechism teaches Christ's Ascension marks the definitive entrance of Jesus' humanity into God's heavenly domain. The Augsburg Confession Article III says he ascended into heaven that he might sit on the right hand of the Father and forever reign over all creatures. The Anglican Thirty-Nine Articles Article IV says Christ rose with his body, with flesh, bones, and all things appertaining to the perfection of Man's nature, wherewith he ascended into Heaven, and there sitteth. The Methodist Articles of Religion say the same in Wesley's abridgement. The Westminster Confession, Heidelberg Catechism, Belgic Confession, and Savoy Declaration affirm the bodily ascension explicitly. The Eastern Orthodox tradition (Orthodox Church in America, for example) affirms the ascension as the entry of Christ's human nature into divine glory while cautioning against picturing a material throne in created space, which is a nuance about heaven, not a denial of the event. Assemblies of God and broader Pentecostal traditions affirm the bodily ascension to the right hand of God. The Dordrecht Confession of the Anabaptists likewise affirms. Public-record denials come from groups historic Christianity has identified as outside its boundary. Jehovah's Witnesses (Watchtower Society, founded by Charles Taze Russell) teach Christ was raised as a spirit creature and the ascension was an invisible spiritual event. Christadelphians teach a bodily resurrection but deny the prior deity of Christ, which collapses the meaning of the ascended Lord. Christian Science (Mary Baker Eddy) denies the literal bodily resurrection and ascension as material events. Liberal demythologizing scholarship (the Bultmann school) recasts the Lukan ascension narratives as theological packaging rather than historical event. Mormonism affirms a physical post-resurrection ministry of Christ but inside a non-trinitarian Christology that distorts the meaning of his enthronement at the Father's right hand.

scripture.key_term: ἐξάγω exago G1806 (led out); ἐπαίρω epairo G1869 (lifted up); εὐλογέω eulogeo G2127 (blessed); διΐστημι diistemi G1339 (parted from); ἀναφέρω anaphero G399 (was carried up)
scripture.force: He led them out as far as Bethany, lifted up his hands, blessed them, and while he was blessing them he parted (diestē) from them and was carried up (anephereto, imperfect passive) into heaven. The passive verbs name the action as done to him by the Father. The participants are physical: hands lifted, eyes seeing. The textual variant anephereto eis ton ouranon is well-attested in NA28 and read by virtually all modern critical editions.

scripture.key_term: ἐπαίρω epairō G1869 (was lifted up, epērthē aorist passive); βλέπω blepō G991 (looking on, present participle); νεφέλη nephelē G3507 (cloud); ὑπολαμβάνω hypolambanō G5274 (received him up)
scripture.force: While they were looking on (blepontōn autōn, genitive absolute, eyewitness participle), he was lifted up (epērthē, aorist passive of epairō, divine passive), and a cloud received him up (hypelaben) from their sight. Three explicit visual-witness markers in one verse. The cloud is OT theophany language (Ex 13:21, Ex 40:34, Dan 7:13) marking divine presence and divine action.

scripture.key_term: θεάομαι theaomai G2300 (beheld); πορεύομαι poreuomai G4198 (going); ἀναλαμβάνω analambanō G353 (taken up, analēmphtheis perfect passive participle); τρόπος tropos G5158 (manner); ἔρχομαι erchomai G2064 (will come, eleusetai future middle)
scripture.force: Two angels say: this same Jesus (houtos ho Iēsous) who was taken up (analēmphtheis, perfect passive: completed act with abiding state) from you into heaven will come (eleusetai) in the same manner (hon tropon) as you have beheld (etheasasthe, aorist middle of theaomai, the same root as theatēs spectator) him going (poreuomenon) into heaven. The angelic interpretation lexically welds the manner of the ascent to the manner of the return. Denying the bodily visible ascent destabilizes the bodily visible return.

scripture.key_term: ἡμέρα hēmera G2250 (days); τεσσαράκοντα tessarakonta G5062 (forty); ὀπτάνομαι optanomai G3700 (appeared)
scripture.force: Jesus presented himself alive to the apostles by many proofs (tekmēriois G5039), being seen by them during forty days (di hēmerōn tessarakonta). This grounds the forty-day interval named in the question statement. Optanomai is visual appearance terminology. The forty-day window is bracketed by resurrection appearances and the visible ascension event.

scripture.key_term: ἀναβαίνω anabainō G305 (ascended); ὕψος hypsos G5311 (high place); καταβαίνω katabainō G2597 (descended); ὑπεράνω hyperanō G5231 (far above); πληρόω plēroō G4137 (might fill)
scripture.force: When he ascended on high (anabas eis hypsos), he led a host of captives. He who descended (ho katabas) is the one who also ascended (anabas) far above all the heavens (hyperanō pantōn tōn ouranōn), that he might fill (hina plērōsē) all things. Paul reads Ps 68:18 christologically. The contrast katebē/anabas frames the ascension as the upper arc of the incarnational movement: same person descended and ascended.

scripture.key_term: ἀναλαμβάνω analambanō G353 (was taken up, anelēmphthē aorist passive); δόξα doxa G1391 (glory)
scripture.force: Confessedly great is the mystery of godliness: he was manifested in the flesh, vindicated in the spirit, seen by angels, proclaimed among the nations, believed on in the world, taken up (anelēmphthē) in glory. Early Christian hymn-fragment listing the ascension as a constitutive gospel-event. The lemma analambanō here is the same root used five times for Christ's ascension across the NT.

scripture.key_term: καθίζω kathizō G2523 (sat down, ekathisen aorist); δεξιά dexia G1188 (right hand); μεγαλωσύνη megalōsynē G3172 (Majesty); ὑψηλά hypsēla G5308 (on high)
scripture.force: After making purification for sins, he sat down (ekathisen) at the right hand of the Majesty on high (en dexia tēs megalōsynēs en hypsēlois). The seating motif presupposes prior physical arrival. Hebrews repeats this seating in 1:13, 8:1, 10:12, 12:2: the Son's enthronement at the Father's right hand is the post-ascension state. The aorist ekathisen marks a completed entry into the heavenly throne-room.

scripture.key_term: ἀναλαμβάνω analambanō G353 (was taken up, anelēmphthē); οὐρανός ouranos G3772 (heaven); καθίζω kathizō G2523 (sat down)
scripture.force: So the Lord Jesus, after speaking to them, was taken up (anelēmphthē) into heaven and sat down (ekathisen) at the right hand of God. Mark's longer ending pairs the ascent verb (analambanō) with the throne-seating verb (kathizō) in one clause. Lexical concordance: same two verbs reappear across Acts, Hebrews, Ephesians 1:20.

scripture.key_term: ὑψόω hypsoō G5312 (exalted, hypsōtheis aorist passive participle); δεξιά dexia G1188; ἀναβαίνω anabainō G305
scripture.force: Peter at Pentecost: being therefore exalted (hypsōtheis) at the right hand of God. v.34: David did not ascend (anebē) into the heavens, but he himself says, the Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand. Peter argues from Ps 110:1 that Jesus, unlike David, did literally ascend bodily and is now seated. The contrastive argument requires a literal physical ascent.

scripture.key_term: ἀτενίζω atenizō G816 (gazed intently); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; δόξα doxa G1391; ἵστημι histēmi G2476 (standing); δεξιός dexios G1188
scripture.force: Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God and Jesus standing at the right hand of God. He says, I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God. Independent eyewitness confirmation: post-ascension, Stephen sees Jesus (in his glorified humanity, Son of Man) at the right hand. The body is locatable in the heavenly throne-room.

scripture.key_term: ἐγείρω egeirō G1453 (raised); καθίζω kathizō G2523 (seated); δεξιά dexia G1188; ἐπουράνιος epouranios G2032 (heavenly)
scripture.force: God raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly places (en tois epouraniois). Paul names the resurrection and the enthronement as one paired action. The phrase en tois epouraniois throughout Ephesians (1:3, 1:20, 2:6, 3:10, 6:12) locates Christ in the heavenly realm.

scripture.key_term: ἄνω anō G507 (above); καθίζω kathizō G2523; δεξιά dexia G1188
scripture.force: If then you have been raised with Christ, seek the things that are above (ta anō), where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God. Paul builds Christian ethics on the location of the ascended Christ. Anō (above) names a real place where Christ presently is.

scripture.key_term: πορεύομαι poreuomai G4198 (gone); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; δεξιά dexia G1188
scripture.force: Jesus Christ, who has gone (poreutheis) into heaven and is at the right hand of God, with angels, authorities, and powers having been subjected to him. Peter reuses poreuomai (the same verb Acts 1:11 uses for the ascension motion: poreuomenon eis ton ouranon).

scripture.key_term: διέρχομαι dierchomai G1330 (passed through); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; ἀρχιερεύς archiereus G749 (high priest)
scripture.force: We have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens (dielēlythota tous ouranous), Jesus the Son of God. The perfect participle marks a completed passage with abiding effect. The ascension is the high-priestly entry into the heavenly sanctuary.

scripture.key_term: εἰσέρχομαι eiserchomai G1525 (entered); οὐρανός ouranos G3772; ἐμφανίζω emphanizō G1718 (appear)
scripture.force: Christ entered (eisēlthen, aorist) not into holy places made with hands but into heaven itself, now to appear (emphanisthēnai) in the presence of God on our behalf. The ascension is named as the literal entry of the high priest into the true sanctuary.

scripture.key_term: ἀναβαίνω anabainō G305 (ascending, anabainō present and anabebēka perfect); πατήρ patēr G3962
scripture.force: Do not cling to me, for I have not yet ascended (anabebēka, perfect: not yet completed) to the Father; but go to my brothers and say to them, I am ascending (anabainō, present durative) to my Father and your Father. Jesus speaks of his ascent to the Father as a literal pending event distinct from the resurrection. The Greek tenses contrast a not-yet-complete state with an in-progress movement.

scripture.key_term: ἀναβαίνω anabainō G305; υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου huios tou anthrōpou G5207 G444 (Son of Man)
scripture.force: What if you were to see the Son of Man ascending (anabainonta) where he was before? Jesus anticipates a visible ascent. Theaomai vocabulary (seeing) is implied; the conditional is a literal sight-event.

scripture.key_term: ὑπερυψόω hyperypsoō G5251 (highly exalted); χαρίζομαι charizomai G5483 (granted); ὄνομα onoma G3686; γόνυ gony G1119 (knee); ἐπουράνιος epouranios G2032
scripture.force: God highly exalted him (hyperypsōsen) and bestowed on him the name above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow in heaven (epouraniōn) and on earth and under the earth. The exaltation is the post-ascension state. Hyperypsoō intensifies hypsoō (the verb Peter uses Acts 2:33 for the ascension-enthronement).

scripture.key_term: כְּבַר אֱנָשׁ kebar enash A1247 A606 (one like a son of man); עֲנָן anan A6050 (clouds); קֳדָם qodam A6925 (before); עַתִּיק יוֹמַיָּא attiq yomayya (Ancient of Days)
scripture.force: I saw in the night visions, and behold, with the clouds of heaven there came one like a son of man, and he came to the Ancient of Days. To him was given dominion. The OT pattern: a son of man figure arrives at the heavenly throne with the clouds. Acts 1:9 reuses the cloud motif (nephelē hypelaben auton). Jesus self-applies Dan 7 in Matt 26:64 (you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand and coming with the clouds).

scripture.key_term: נְאֻם ne'um H5002 (declaration); אָדוֹן adon H113 (Lord); יָמִין yamin H3225 (right hand); שָׁבַת yashab H3427 (sit)
scripture.force: The LORD says to my Lord: sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool. Most-quoted OT verse in the NT (cited or alluded to 25+ times). The Father invites the ascended Son to be seated at his right hand. The very imagery presupposes literal arrival in the heavenly throne-room.

scripture.key_term: עָלָה alah H5927 (ascended); מָרוֹם marom H4791 (on high); שָׁבָה shavah H7617 (led captive); שְׁבִי shevi H7628 (captivity)
scripture.force: You ascended on high, leading a host of captives in your train. Quoted in Eph 4:8 as the OT source for the ascension motif. Alah lemarom (went up to the height) is the Hebrew counterpart to anabas eis hypsos. The bodily-ascent vocabulary is rooted in OT enthronement imagery.

scripture.key_term: סוּפָה sufah H5492 (whirlwind); עָלָה alah H5927 (went up); הַשָּׁמָיִם hashamayim H8064 (the heavens)
scripture.force: Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven (vaya'al Eliyahu basse'arah hashamayim). OT typological precedent for a visible bodily translation into heaven, witnessed by Elisha. The verb alah (go up) is the Hebrew equivalent of anabainō. Establishes the canonical pattern that the New Testament fulfills in Christ.

hermeneutics.notes: Grammatico-historical reading of the canonical lexical pattern is sufficient and decisive. No interpretive framework (covenantal, dispensational, new-covenant theology, progressive covenantalism) produces a different verdict; bodily ascension sits underneath every framework as part of the apostolic kerygma. Analogia scripturae is heavily invoked: the verdict rests on a pan-canonical chain (Luke 24, Acts 1, Acts 2, Acts 7, Eph 1, Eph 4, Phil 2, Col 3, 1 Tim 3, Heb 1, Heb 4, Heb 9, 1 Pet 3, Mark 16, John 6, John 20) and OT roots (Ps 68:18, Ps 110:1, Dan 7:13-14, 2 Kgs 2:11). Progressive revelation factor: OT seeds the ascent-to-heaven pattern (Enoch translated, Elijah by whirlwind, Daniel's Son of Man arriving on clouds), Christ realizes it as the singular high-priestly enthronement. The seeming complication that heaven is not a spatial coordinate dissolves by reading the bodily-visible-departure at the earthly end and the throne-room-arrival at the heavenly end as both real without forcing the in-between motion into modern physics. No competing-lens verdict survives the canon as a whole.

counter_witness.anchor: Apostles' Creed and Nicene Creed (325/381), universally confessed in the ancient church
counter_witness.key_phrase: He ascended into heaven and sitteth on the right hand of God the Father Almighty; from thence he shall come to judge the quick and the dead.

counter_witness.anchor: Irenaeus, Against Heresies, Book III, especially III.16-19 (CCEL ANF Vol 1)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The same Christ who suffered for us and rose again and ascended into the heavens in the body of his flesh shall come again in the same form in which he was seen by the apostles.

counter_witness.anchor: Augustine, Sermon 263 on the Ascension; City of God Book XXII
counter_witness.key_phrase: Today our Lord Jesus Christ ascended into heaven; let our hearts ascend with him. He did not abandon heaven when he came down to us; nor did he withdraw from us when he went up again.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church, paragraphs 659-667 (Article 6: He Ascended into Heaven)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ's Ascension marks the definitive entrance of Jesus' humanity into God's heavenly domain, whence he will come again, but which in the meantime hides him from the eyes of men.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession (1530), Article III (Of the Son of God), bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: He ascended into heaven that he might sit on the right hand of the Father, and forever reign and have dominion over all creatures.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article IV Of the Resurrection of Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ did truly rise again from death, and took again his body, with flesh, bones, and all things appertaining to the perfection of Man's nature; wherewith he ascended into Heaven, and there sitteth.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter 8 paragraph 4; Heidelberg Catechism Lord's Day 18 (Q&A 46-49)
counter_witness.key_phrase: On the third day He arose from the dead, with the same body in which He suffered; with which also He ascended into heaven, and there sitteth at the right hand of His Father, making intercession.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article III Of the Resurrection of Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ did truly rise again from the dead, and took again his body, with all things appertaining to the perfection of man's nature, wherewith he ascended into heaven.

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith (1632), Article IV (On the Coming of Christ)
counter_witness.key_phrase: He suffered, died, rose again on the third day, ascended to heaven, and now sits at the right hand of the majesty of God in heaven.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 2 (The One True God) and Article 7 (The Promise of the Father)
counter_witness.key_phrase: We believe in his bodily resurrection, his ascension to the right hand of the Father, and his personal future return to earth in power and glory.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith Volume II, Worship: The Feast of the Ascension (oca.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Ascension does not mean that somewhere in the created universe the physical Jesus is sitting on a material throne. It is the entry of Christ's glorified humanity into the eternal life of God.

web.quote: Luke 24:50: he led them out (exēgagen G1806) to Bethany, lifted up (eparas G1869) his hands (cheiras G5495), and blessed (eulogēsen G2127) them. Eyewitness physicality.
Acts 1:9: while they were looking on (blepontōn autōn), he was lifted up (epērthē G1869, divine passive), and a cloud (nephelē G3507) received him (hypelaben G5274) from their sight.
Acts 1:11: this Jesus, taken up (analēmphtheis G353), will come (eleusetai) in the same manner (hon tropon G5158) as you beheld (etheasasthe G2300) him going (poreuomenon).
Eph 4:10: he who descended (katabas G2597) is the same who ascended (anabas G305) far above (hyperanō G5231) all the heavens, that he might fill (plērōsē G4137) all things.
1 Tim 3:16: was taken up (anelēmphthē G353) in glory (doxē G1391). The hymn-fragment names the ascension as gospel-content alongside manifestation in flesh and proclamation to nations.
Heb 1:3: after making purification for sins, he sat down (ekathisen G2523) at the right hand (en dexia G1188) of the Majesty (megalōsynēs G3172) on high (en hypsēlois G5308).
Analambanō G353: to take up, raise. Used five times of Christ's ascension across Mark 16:19, Acts 1:2, Acts 1:11, Acts 1:22, 1 Tim 3:16. Five distinct contexts, one lexical formula.
Anabainō G305: to go up, ascend. Used of Christ's ascent in John 3:13, John 6:62, John 20:17, Eph 4:8-10. Always literal upward motion when paired with eis ton ouranon.
Methodist Article III: Christ did truly rise again from the dead, and took again his body, with all things appertaining to the perfection of man's nature, wherewith he ascended into heaven.
CCC 665: Christ's Ascension marks the definitive entrance of Jesus' humanity into God's heavenly domain, whence he will come again, but which in the meantime hides him from the eyes of men.
Augsburg Confession Article III: He ascended into heaven that He might sit on the right hand of the Father, and forever reign and have dominion over all creatures.
OCA: the Ascension does not mean that somewhere in the created universe the physical Jesus is sitting in a material throne. It is the entry of Christ's glorified humanity into divine life.
